era_context: The Late Bronze Age collapse is approximately 150–200 years in the future. The world is dominated by the New Kingdom of Egypt (19th Dynasty under Ramesses II or his successors), the Hittite Empire in Anatolia, Mycenaean Greece, the Shang Dynasty in China, and various Levantine city-states. Long-distance trade networks (tin, cedar, lapis lazuli) are robust. Writing systems include Egyptian hieroglyphics, Hittite cuneiform, Linear B in Greece, and oracle bone script in China.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (limited synchronisation with Old World chronologies)
Southeast Asia and Oceania (sparse archaeological dating for this specific year)

events.title: Reign of Ramesses II (Usermaatra-setep-en-re) — continued military and building campaigns
events.description: Ramesses II, one of Egypt's longest-reigning pharaohs, continues his extensive building programme and military consolidation. By this date (approximately year 10–15 of his reign, depending on chronological system), he has already fought the Battle of Kadesh (c. 1274 BCE in conventional dating) and is consolidating control over Nubia and the Levant. His reign is marked by monumental construction (Abu Simbel, Karnak expansions) and the maintenance of Egyptian hegemony in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and reliefs (Abu Simbel, Karnak, Abydos)

events.certainty_note: Ramesses II's reign is well-attested, but precise year-by-year dating within his 66-year reign depends on which chronological system is used (conventional, low, or high chronology). This year falls within his documented reign with high confidence, but specific events for this exact year are not always distinguishable.
cross_references: Battle of Kadesh (c. 1274 BCE, earlier in his reign)
Construction of Abu Simbel (c. 1264 BCE, later in his reign)

events.title: Hittite Empire under Muwatalli II or successor — consolidation in Anatolia and Syria
events.description: The Hittite Empire, centred at Hattusa, maintains its position as a major Bronze Age power. By this date, the empire is either under Muwatalli II (if dating to c. 1295–1272 BCE) or his successor Mursili III. The Hittites control extensive territories in Anatolia and contest Egyptian influence in the Levant. Trade and diplomatic correspondence with Egypt, Mycenae, and Babylon are active.
key_figures: Muwatalli II or Mursili III (Hittite Great King)

sources.name: Amarna Letters (diplomatic correspondence, slightly earlier period)

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology is debated; conventional dating places this period within the reign of Muwatalli II or his immediate successor, but uncertainty of ±10–15 years is common for this era.

events.title: Shang Dynasty oracle bone divination and ritual practices — continued
events.description: The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) continues its ritual and administrative practices. Oracle bones (scapulae and turtle plastrons) are inscribed with divination records, providing evidence of Shang governance, warfare, and religious beliefs. By this period, the Shang have established a sophisticated bronze-working culture and maintain a hierarchical state structure. Writing is well-developed, though limited to elite contexts.
key_figures: Shang king (specific ruler uncertain for this year)
events.certainty_note: Shang chronology is uncertain; conventional dating places this year within the Shang Dynasty, but specific year-by-year attribution is not possible from available sources. Oracle bones are dated by stratigraphy and typology, with uncertainties of ±20–50 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of Ramesses II's reign (c. 1279–1213 BCE) is disputed by some scholars who favour a 'low chronology' (shifting dates down by 50–100 years) or 'high chronology' (shifting up). This affects the precise year-by-year alignment of Egyptian events with other civilisations. Additionally, the identification of specific Hittite rulers for this year depends on which chronological system is adopted, and some scholars dispute the conventional sequence. No disconfirming evidence undermines the general characterisation of this era as a period of major Bronze Age empires, but precise synchronisation across regions remains contested.
historiographic_note: This period is well-documented for Egypt and the Hittite Empire through inscriptions, administrative records, and archaeological evidence. However, synchronisation across regions depends heavily on conventional chronologies, which are themselves debated. Chinese sources (oracle bones) are abundant but difficult to date precisely. The historical record is biased towards elite, literate societies; vast regions of Africa, Europe, and the Americas are essentially invisible in written sources. The 'Bronze Age world' described here is a construct of literate empires; most human populations left no written record.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses II's reign continues into subsequent years with ongoing building and military campaigns
Hittite and Egyptian empires coexist as major powers, with diplomatic and military rivalry
